item.title: This Made Me Cry
item.content:encoded:
I loved this so much I bothered to finally get my account validated so I could +1 it.

It has incredibly solid storytelling, the characters feel real, the jargon is a bit tough to parse but still very digestible, and all in a very concise format. Plus, you managed to tell a positive hopeful story on the SCPWiki, which is always a nice breath of fresh air.

This is why you're one of my favorite writers on here, Hippo.

item.title: Witnessing a Miracle
item.content:encoded:
It's so crazy. I've got a solid toe in chemistry and industry. I know these characters. I've seen them and I've met them. Others have already said the documentary-esque feel of this is just… Powerful. What gets me, what really just… blows the baseline of something so seemingly mundane out of the water-compared to things like time loops, evil flesh, and cross-dimensional strip clubs-is just how REAL it is. I could see this happening.

You said that there was a similar event. I could see THIS event happening. And, I know enough about metallurgy and the craziness of how wild steel production can be. Three pieces of pipe with a slightly higher vanadium content mix with a few old plow sheers that had higher carbon content and the sun was hotter that day so the crystalization of the steel shifted just enough. All the steel in the batch met requirements so who cares if it has a higher melt temp. Say in the end 14 beams have high enough melting points to do this and most of them will never matter. They were built unimportant structures in unimportant places. But this one? This miracle I-beam? This thing no one could predict? Right where it needs to be.

It could happen. I can't tell you without checking, that this story DIDN'T happen EXACTLY this way. Paranormal stories? Most cannot hold a candle to that. And there are plenty of great ways to tell a story where something crazy happened… But to make it THAT real? That's good stuff man. Gods that's good.

item.title: wow
item.content:encoded:
Jesus. Those stories of people who know they're gonna die and keep on fighting anyway really speak to me, and you captured that perfectly. It has that exact sort of character of something like the Bhopal disaster. +1

P.S. That song you linked is amazing. Thanks for reminding me of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a captivating read from start to finish. The jargon doesn’t distract from the story at all. I felt this was something that definitely needed a top-quality Japanese translation, so here it is:
http://scp-jp.wikidot.com/scp-3352
Hippo how can every single article of yours be so good??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
USCSB is wonderful. Here's the USCSB video of the explosion this article is actually based on.

ETA: Anyone with any experience in a refinery will likely feel their asshole pucker at the phrase "explosion in the alkylation unit". Nevermind "causing the largest explosion".

ETA Part 2: And this video isn't what this article is based on, but wins my prize for "most likely to give Hippo a fucking panic attack as it goes on".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CSB SCP CSB SCP CSB SCP CSB SCP

superb. +1

Edit: anyone who enjoys the dissection of a fictional process plant disaster will likely thoroughly enjoy the United States Chemical Safety Board's Youtube channel, full of reconstruction documentaries of various non-fictional disasters. (Spoiler: 99% of the time it's because management tried to save money by skimping on safety, in whatever form that takes for the facility in question.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not what I'd expect from a mainlist article, and that's not necessarily a bad thing, but it did leave me thinking "okay, okay, when are we going to get to the point?"

The interspersal of dry descriptions with interviews doesn't feel very professional: I'd expect a full synopsis of the events, and then the interviews. The way it is right now, it reads like a documentary script.

That said, it's a very well-written documentary. Good job and thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this one. It's not typical for SCP but it doesn't have to be. Instead of being about something weird and aggressively anomalous we get something that feels like it could have happened. This feels like one of those miracle stories old people tell on tv when a disaster happens, but in SCP form. Grabs attention and stands out from the pack because of it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing Story in this article, it really developed well and I think it worked perfectly to end up finally showing why nothing broke because of an indestructible I beam. It was really entertaining the whole time and while there’s some things that I think could possibly be better the story makes it amazing no matter what small errors you may of made. Also I kept thinking of Chernobyl while I was reading this, anyway +1 and my favorite series 4 article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely fantastic. I've always been a sucker for skips that can make something relatively mundane (a steel beam that didn't break when a non-anomalous one should've) into a big deal. It really puts the far more directly powerful skips in perspective.

And, of course, it tells a really, really engaging story about a realistic chemical plant failure, which is something I've never thought about before.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knowing a bit of chemistry, this is one of the most terrifying articles on the wiki and the actual SCP has nothing to do with the horror. Absolutely stellar writing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really sweet story, almost reminds me of those stories you'd see on Beyond Belief. I was honestly worried all the jargon would muddle it up, but it ended up being really important to understanding the story and why the beam was an anomaly. Maybe it's a bit too saccharine, but I think stories like this are what SCP should be all about.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ (no pun intended), I think I have a new favorite article…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe I'm just a sucker for scips that don't try to hide anything, but this definitely is one of my favorite SCPs. There was a very real feeling about this article, especially because of how similar it is to actual events. Something felt very human about being so determined to help others that a miracle happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ohhh, this is a good one.

I've actually spent a lot of my own time thinking "wow, USCSB videos all seem like prime SCP fuel, but how do you make fiction that's more compelling than the reality of chemical disasters?"

You did it. This is a captivating, concise, and intense read. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending was magical

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who studied chemical engineering for two and a half years before changing majors (really, I'm not the type who wants to be responsible for the lives of those whom my work affects), this one hit me really hard.

+1 for solid impact, and for believable post-incident testimony. This may actually be my new favorite entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very easy +1. Aside from 4999 I think this is the only article on this site that's made me cry. I think for both articles, there's something about the concept of ordinary people, going through something extremely difficult, getting an impossible kindness that's very touching. You can just see Watkins in that pipe alley, hoping beyond any hope that that beam won't collapse… and then it doesn't. It's really powerful!

A lot of fiction tries to pull off that emotional beat and it winds up being cheesy, or you don't believe the stakes, but I think you executed it really well. Aside from that, basing it off of a real explosion was great for verisimilitude, you can tell it's well-researched. As others have said, it really feels just like a documentary, cutting between footage and later interviews with the survivors. I also think all the interviewees had distinct voices without distracting from the story.

Instant top-5 favorite. I hope this gets a lot more attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really incredible. It shows the stakes in a way anyone can appreciate but escalates the events and ends with the most trivial anomaly that has a fantastic in-universe and narrative impact. When it started talking about HF as more than just a detail I knew this was going to be terrible. The danger despite realism really did this for me.

It's also quite interesting for me knowing some of the similarities to real life events, and just how messed up and complicated these structures are.

Great piece, well deserving +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was fantastic. I tend to have a soft spot for more industrial themes in writing, so I was optimistic from the start, but wow, this really knocks it out of the park. There's something really striking here about, in a story about things going incredibly wrong, the actual anomaly being something deceptively simple but ultimately life-saving. I'm not sure if I can quite articulate it, but something about the, well, scary parts of this article being the realistic ones for a change just make it hit all the harder. Great writing as always and an easy +1.

This already did way better than I expected it to, so I figured I'd explain a couple of things for anyone curious:

AUTHOR'S NOTES
– hide block
  • The story is semi-real (based off an actual I-beam that held up a line when it had every reason to fail). The only real difference are the stakes — if the I-beam had fallen, it would have killed everyone on the unit (not everyone in the city).
  • Hydrofluoric alkylation units are very real, and they are very terrifying.
  • The only reason Jefferson Reeds says hydrofluoric acid wouldn't make number 1 in 'Worst Chemicals to Work With' is because things like dimethylmercury exist. If you're curious to read more about horrifying chemistry, here's a great blog on that very subject (thanks to Jekeled for reminding me that it exists).
  • This is also based off the explosion in the Philadelphia Refinery in 2019 — which, yes, happened in an HF unit — and yes, they had an emergency shutdown system (which, thank God, worked precisely as intended).
  • In the story, Daniel Ruhl refuses to open the hydrant-line because ice on a hydrant in July means something is very wrong. He just hadn't figured out what, yet.
  • As for backing liquid propane into your fire-suppression system… yes, it can happen (and yes, it has happened). Ice on your hydrants is one of the big warning signs.
  • It's cheesy as fuck, but I wrote this story in a fugue state after hearing We All Lift Together — specifically, the line: Feel the weight of what we owe. When you build a refinery in the heart of a city, you're putting the people of that city directly in the line of fire. Something is owed. The stakeholders, owners, and supervisors might not feel the weight of that debt — but someone has to.
  • When Tanya Sullivan says 'we'd be damned if we let this place take anyone else with it', she's thinking literally: the steel beam didn't just save the city; it spared the workers the guilt of having killed an entire city's worth of people. In her mind, and in the mind of Matthew Watkins, that beam saved their souls.
  • Ultimately, the only real anomaly here… is that this refinery did not kill the city.

item.title: Re: what ruined it for me
item.content:encoded:
It's neutralized because the phenomenon happened once, then never again. Now it's just an ordinary steel I-beam.

ETA: This is why the anomaly isn't the steel I-beam itself, but rather, what happened with it.

item.title: what ruined it for me
item.content:encoded:
what ruined it for me:
"Neutralized"

Is that a chemistry joke? An oversight? It seems pretty darn anomalous that the fundation didn't try to replicate such a useful material…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but imagining an unbreakable, glowing, hot "I" beam manifesting from the workers' determination to save the city made me cry tears of joy. Heh, the song helped a lot too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for taking the time to reply!

To be honest, this had made the events in the SCP even more terrifying. I'll be sure to keep an eye out for any ice before opening firehydrants in the future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And it absolutely is not anomalous; this is a thing that can happen (and has). It's why you're not supposed to hook fire hydrants up to anything without a lot of safeguards in place — and why if you see a fire hydrant with ice on it in the middle of July, you ought to step back and think before you decide to plug in a hose and open it up.

ETA: To clarify, what happened was someone tried to use a fire-hydrant to flush out a big tank, thinking there was nothing in it… but the tank was full of high-pressure liquid propane. Instead of water going into the propane tank, the propane pushed into the water-line — dumping propane into the refinery's fire-suppression system.

And to further clarify, this is NOT something that I could imagine happening outside of a refinery. Though if you see an ordinary fire hydrant on a city-street iced over in the middle of July… I'd still want some questions answered before deciding to open it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, I absolutely love this. The descriptions are evocative and intense. An enthusiastic +1.

One question, sorry if the answer is there and I missed it: was the propane in the water anomalous, or something that just happened / could happen? Either way, a terrifying image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The resulting detonation killed the operator on the unit and ejected burning debris as far as 5km, narrowly missing several homes in the surrounding city.

You added this line in revisions since I first read it, but I think it dilutes the theme a little. The overall idea of the article is a repeating series of catastrophic failures, one after the other, until finally at the last moment there's a miracle. Except there's a moment early on where things don't go as poorly as they could have, and tragedy is averted. This introduces the idea that there might have been a second miracle, or that the miracle is greater than the I-Beam failing to melt. Neither of these is particularly strong to me.

Also I got the sense that the anomaly was created by the inscription. Having another near-miss, completely unrelated to the inscription or the steel, really takes away that sense of origin.

EDIT: The line above has been removed, so I should state my feelings on this: I absolutely love this. This is one of my favorite articles on the site. The imagery is incredibly vivid, and that last scene of watching a single I-Beam hold up the heavens is exceptional.

EDIT 2: I just realized something — the I-Beam with the inscription was covered in concrete before the plant started to melt down. Since it's obvious that nobody wrote that inscription on it at the time (Matthew couldn't have, since his palms had been burnt off, and the fire monitors were pointing fire directly at the pillars), the inscription had to have been on the concrete before it burnt off. The image of everything going to shit and the concrete getting blasted off, only for the blessing to then become visible is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! The decision to not explain what the anomaly was until the very end was probably the riskiest part of this — but also the first thing I recognized the article needed to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a wonderful piece of storytelling. There is enough exposition to make the mechanics and stakes clear to a layman reader without getting tripped up in pedantry. I wasn't sure about the decision to not start with a description of the anomaly, but it was the right decision: the ending doesn't just show what the anomaly is, it shows what it means. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! Also, something to consider (and part of what I was aiming to achieve):

Ultimately, the real anomaly is that this horrible industrial disaster did NOT kill ten thousand people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the way you set this up. I’m reading about an industrial disaster, carnage, and human error, wondering, what’s the catch? What’s weird? And at the very end you nail it down! Or I beam it I suppose. So simple, but great story surrounding a rather unremarkable anomaly in and of itself and made it a center of importance to the piece!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made a few changes to address this (and also hammer home one of the central themes of the piece I was aiming for). Hopefully, the changes help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The action becomes difficult to follow and visualize around the midway point of the article, but the frisson of the climax still hits like a truck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing the sentiment re: this reading like an extended USCSB video. Hell, I'm pretty sure I remember bits of this from various videos: propane in place of water, ice on the hydrants…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can just see the sweeping, panning zooms, smooth rendered environments, little hard-hatted blank figures and cross-section cutaways in my head when I read. And you got me empathizing with a steel beam. If I hadn't watched tons of USCSB videos I guess I wouldn't appreciate it as much, but combined with the visual evocativitiy, the fact that it almost feels like a story I'd read on an ill-reputed strange phenomena youtube channel and still find myself believing is.

Impressive.

easy +1 ofc

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so great, I love how the entire story doesn't really feel anomalous, its just a dreadful story of how everything goes wrong. But then the end hit me. Really interesting overall, especially loved the "outta nowhere, its day again." Practically knew i was gonna be upvoting when i read that line. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This very much feels like the kind of incident that turns a man religious. Just the image of someone standing there, betting their life on a steel beam, and then being rewarded for it is very visceral. A+ work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd been going back and forth on this for a while, but you've tipped me over to thinking that yeah, just letting it end on 'no structural damage' is the best choice (it's already stated that there's nothing remarkable about the I-beam).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite this incident, no anomalous properties have been observed.

In my opinion, this is a bad stinger. The "no structural damage" line ends the SCP much better. That being said, this is a great exercise in prose that I enjoyed.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately, I didn't find this one appealing. The vast majority of the article is taken up by the description of a meltdown at the refinery, which while I suppose would be horrific, keeps you wondering through the article how this is supposed to be anomalous or linked to the Foundation. We finally learn about it at the end, but it doesn't really come soon enough for the article, at least for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing plays out like a well-crafted documentary, making a seemingly impenetrable subject come across clearly. Combined with the tight mystery and just the right amount of spookiness makes it a great read.

item.title: <insert tasteless 'jet fuel can't melt steel beams' joke here>
item.content:encoded:
PUSH: To keep the dark from coming.
FEEL: The weight of what we owe.
THIS: The song of sons and daughters.
HIDE: The heart of who we are.

Making peace to build a future,
Strong united, working till we fall.

And we all lift...

—We All Lift Together (Keith Power)

A story about everything that could possibly go wrong, going wrong — except the one thing that didn't.

SOURCES:

  • Link here. All other images are mine; I release them under CC 3.0.
  • Inspired by real events surrounding a real steel I-beam.

I don't expect this to be a popular one. It's one of those things I just had to write out for myself, to get it out of my head — me posting it is more a formality than anything else.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
